content.text: Initiative parameters are declared at the policySetDefinition level and flow down into individual policy definition references inside the initiative. They serve as the single control surface for anyone assigning the initiative — instead of configuring each constituent policy separately, the assignee sets initiative-level values that propagate to all relevant policies at evaluation time. An initiative can have up to 100 parameters.

attrs.alt: Three initiative parameter patterns: threaded, hardcoded, effect switch

content.text: The key distinction from individual policy parameters is scope: an initiative parameter is declared once and can be wired into multiple policy references within the same initiative. For example, a single logAnalyticsWorkspaceId initiative parameter can flow into five different AINE and DINE policy references that each need a workspace ID — the assignee supplies the value once at assignment time.

Initiative parameters support the same type set as individual policy parameters. The type determines how the Azure Portal renders the input field and what validation ARM applies when the assignment is created.

The strongType metadata hint tells the Azure Portal to render a resource picker for that parameter field. Common values: omsWorkspace (Log Analytics workspace picker), location, virtualNetwork, subnet, storageAccount. Without strongType, the Portal renders a plain text input.

Each policy definition reference inside the initiative has a parameters block that maps the policy's own parameters to values. Three forms are possible: an initiative parameter reference (the value is [parameters('initiativeParamName')]), a hardcoded value, or a field() function reference for resource properties. The field() form is rarely used in initiative parameters but is valid for passing resource-level values to policies that accept them.

A single initiative parameter can be wired to any number of policy references within the initiative. This is the primary value of initiative-level parameters — supplying one value at assignment time that flows to every policy that needs it. The most common example is a Log Analytics workspace ID that needs to reach all AINE and DINE policies in the initiative simultaneously.
Managing Initiative Parameters with Azure CLI

# View all parameters defined in an initiative
az policy set-definition show \
  --name corp-security-baseline \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --query 'parameters' -o json

# View parameter values currently set on an assignment
az policy assignment show \
  --name corp-baseline \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --query 'parameters' -o json

# Update a single parameter value on an existing assignment
az policy assignment update \
  --name corp-baseline \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --set 'parameters.auditEffect.value=Disabled'

# Update the workspace ID (e.g., after workspace migration)
NEW_LAW_ID='/subscriptions/11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111/resourceGroups/rg-monitoring/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/law-corp-new'

az policy assignment update \
  --name corp-baseline \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --set "parameters.logAnalyticsWorkspaceId.value=$NEW_LAW_ID"

# Trigger a compliance re-scan to evaluate with the new workspace ID
az policy state trigger-scan --management-group mg-corp

# Verify the new workspace ID is in use across all constituent policies
az policy state list \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "policySetDefinitionId contains 'corp-security-baseline'" \
  --query '[?policyDefinitionAction==`auditifnotexists`].{RefId:policyDefinitionReferenceId,State:complianceState}' \
  -o table
Most parameters — let assignments override; defaultValue ensures assignment still works if value omitted
Every policy effect — enables per-environment toggle via assignment parameters
Values that are the same everywhere and should not vary by assignment (e.g., tag naming schema)
Allowed regions, allowed SKUs, allowed image publishers
Retention days, count thresholds, timeout values
Terraform: Full Initiative with Parameterized Policy References

When an assignment is created, Azure Resource Manager validates that all required parameters (those without a defaultValue) have been supplied, and that supplied values satisfy any allowedValues constraints. If the initiative parameter declaration includes an allowedValues list and the assignment provides a value outside that list, the assignment creation fails with a validation error. This validation happens synchronously — the assignment is rejected before it is persisted.

# Attempt to create an assignment with an invalid effect value
az policy assignment create \
  --name corp-baseline \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --policy-set-definition $INITIATIVE_ID \
  --params '{"denyPublicStorageEffect": {"value": "Block"}}'
# Error: The parameter 'denyPublicStorageEffect' has a value of 'Block' which is not
# in the list of allowed values: ['Audit', 'Deny', 'Disabled'].

# Attempt to create an assignment without a required parameter
az policy assignment create \
  --name corp-baseline \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --policy-set-definition $INITIATIVE_ID
  # (no --params supplied)
# Error: The policy assignment is missing required parameters: logAnalyticsWorkspaceId

# View parameter validation errors in the REST response (useful for scripting)
# Use the REST API directly to see structured error detail:
az rest \
  --method put \
  --uri "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/corp-baseline?api-version=2022-06-01" \
  --body '{
    "properties": {
      "policyDefinitionId": "$INITIATIVE_ID",
      "parameters": { "logAnalyticsWorkspaceId": { "value": "" } }
    }
  }'
# Returns HTTP 400 with code=InvalidPolicyParameterValue if validation fails

Initiative parameters cannot express conditional relationships between parameters — there is no if/else at the initiative parameter level. If you need a parameter to behave differently based on another parameter's value, you handle this outside the initiative by computing the final value in Terraform locals, an Azure DevOps pipeline variable, or a parameter file before supplying it to the assignment. The initiative itself receives the already-resolved values.

# Pattern: compute effect values outside the initiative based on environment tag
ENVIRONMENT=$(az group show --name rg-prod --query tags.environment -o tsv)

if [ "$ENVIRONMENT" == 'production' ]; then
  DENY_EFFECT='Deny'
  AUDIT_EFFECT='AuditIfNotExists'
elif [ "$ENVIRONMENT" == 'staging' ]; then
  DENY_EFFECT='Audit'
  AUDIT_EFFECT='AuditIfNotExists'
else
  DENY_EFFECT='Disabled'
  AUDIT_EFFECT='Disabled'
fi

# Supply computed values to the assignment
az policy assignment create \
  --name corp-baseline \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --policy-set-definition $INITIATIVE_ID \
  --params "{
    \"logAnalyticsWorkspaceId\": {\"value\": \"$LAW_ID\"},
    \"denyPublicStorageEffect\": {\"value\": \"$DENY_EFFECT\"},
    \"auditVmAgentEffect\": {\"value\": \"$AUDIT_EFFECT\"}
  }"

# Alternative: use a parameters JSON file per environment
# params-prod.json:
# {
#   "logAnalyticsWorkspaceId": {"value": "/subscriptions/.../workspaces/law-prod"},
#   "denyPublicStorageEffect": {"value": "Deny"},
#   "auditVmAgentEffect": {"value": "AuditIfNotExists"}
# }

az policy assignment create \
  --name corp-baseline-prod \
  --scope /subscriptions/$PROD_SUB_ID \
  --policy-set-definition $INITIATIVE_ID \
  --params @params-prod.json

Initiative assignment parameters can be updated at any time without deleting and re-creating the assignment. The update takes effect for new resources immediately and for existing resources after the next compliance scan cycle (typically within 15 minutes for trigger-scan, or up to 24 hours for the automatic background scan). This makes effect parameters useful as a progressive rollout mechanism: start at Audit, monitor for a few weeks, then flip to Deny.

# Progressive rollout: Audit → Deny over 30 days

# Day 1: assign with Audit effect to observe compliance impact
az policy assignment create \
  --name corp-baseline \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --policy-set-definition $INITIATIVE_ID \
  --params '{
    "logAnalyticsWorkspaceId": {"value": "$LAW_ID"},
    "denyPublicStorageEffect": {"value": "Audit"},
    "auditVmAgentEffect": {"value": "AuditIfNotExists"}
  }'

# Day 30: check non-compliant count
az policy state summarize \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "policySetDefinitionId contains 'corp-security-baseline'" \
  --query 'results.resourceDetails'

# Day 30: flip to Deny effect (no assignment delete/recreate — just update)
az policy assignment update \
  --name corp-baseline \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --set 'parameters.denyPublicStorageEffect.value=Deny'

# Verify the update applied
az policy assignment show \
  --name corp-baseline \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --query 'parameters.denyPublicStorageEffect' -o json
# { "value": "Deny" }

# Trigger immediate compliance scan (optional — background scan runs within 24h anyway)
az policy state trigger-scan --management-group mg-corp

Initiative parameters are the primary mechanism for making a single initiative definition work across multiple environments. Thread them to individual policy references so a single assignment value propagates to all constituent policies that need it. Use the effect parameter pattern for every policy in the initiative to enable per-assignment toggling between Deny, Audit, and Disabled. Hardcode values only when they are truly invariant — anything that might differ between dev and production should be a threaded parameter.
